name: Short-term oral rapamycin in older adults was feasible but did not improve function
summary: This older-adult rapamycin trial constrains systemic rapalog and senomorphic extrapolation because it was short, small, and did not improve measured cognitive, physical-performance, self-perceived health, or most immune parameters.
statement: In a 25-participant older-adult pilot trial, 1 mg daily oral rapamycin for at least eight weeks appeared feasible without clinically significant short-term safety effects, but it did not change measured cognitive, physical-performance, self-perceived health, or most immune parameters.
population_or_model: Generally healthy adults aged 70 to 95 years
time_horizon: At least eight weeks of treatment in completers
quantitative_note: The abstract reports 25 randomized participants, three withdrawals, and 11 rapamycin plus 14 control participants included in analysis.
caveats: The trial did not test SASP or senescence biomarkers as the main efficacy endpoint.
Short-term feasibility does not establish long-term preventive safety.
No functional improvement was detected in the measured cognitive or physical-performance endpoints.
